MIG.notes: Electrode positive for standard MIG welding

TIG.notes: DC electrode negative for standard TIG

MIG.weldable_materials: Mild Steel, Stainless Steel, Aluminum (with optional Spool Gun)

TIG.weldable_materials: Mild Steel, Stainless Steel, Chrome Moly

solutions: Increase weld current
Decrease travel speed
Use faster wire feed
Use shorter CTWD
Decrease weld current
Increase travel speed
Use slower wire feed
Use longer CTWD

troubleshooting.issue: Voltage Too Low or Wire Feed Too Slow
solutions: Increase Output Voltage
Increase Wire Feed Speed

troubleshooting.issue: Voltage Too High or Wire Feed Too Fast
solutions: Decrease Output Voltage
Decrease Wire Feed Speed
Travel slower
Travel faster

troubleshooting.issue: CTWD Too Long or Wrong Polarity
solutions: Check Polarity
Maintain less than 1/2 inch CTWD

troubleshooting.description: Weld drops on top and underneath, or falls through entirely, making a hole

solutions: Reduce wire feed speed
Decrease weld current
Increase travel speed and ensure that travel speed is kept steady
Reduce wire feed speed

troubleshooting.description: Weld does not penetrate the joint fully, just sits on the surface

solutions: Maintain 1/2" or less CTWD
Keep arc on leading edge of weld puddle
Hold MIG Gun at proper angles
Reduce travel speed
Increase weld current
Bevel thick workpieces, allow slight gap, and weld in several passes
Increase wire feed speed
Clamp workpieces securely
Make tack welds to hold workpieces
Weld a small portion and allow to cool before proceeding
Increase travel speed
Reduce wire feed speed

troubleshooting.description: Gaps present between weld and previous bead or between weld and workpiece

solutions: Place stringer bead at correct place in joint
Adjust workpiece position or weld angle to permit proper welding to bottom of piece
Pause briefly at sides during weave bead
Keep arc on leading edge of weld puddle
Hold MIG Gun at proper angles
Increase current
Increase wire feed speed
Clean workpiece down to bare metal
Increase wire feed speed
Widen groove or increase bevel

troubleshooting.description: Slag is a necessary part of a flux-cored wire weld. It shields the weld from impurities. Clean off the slag with a Chipping Hammer and Wire Brush after welding. Gas-shielded MIG welds are protected by the shielding gas and do not need slag to protect them.
Small cavities or holes in the bead

causes_and_solutions.solution: Check that polarity is set correctly for type of welding
Increase flow of gas. Clean nozzle. Maintain proper CTWD
Use shielding gas recommended by wire supplier
Clean workpiece down to bare metal. Make certain that wire is clean and free from oil, coatings, and other residues
Maintain steady travel speed
Reduce CTWD

troubleshooting.description: Fine spatter is normal. Spatter that is grainy and large is a problem

causes_and_solutions.solution: Clean workpiece down to bare metal. Make certain that wire is clean and free from oil, coatings, and other residues
Check that polarity is set correctly for type of welding
Increase flow of gas. Clean nozzle. Maintain proper CTWD
Reduce wire feed speed
Reduce CTWD
Use the hands or rest hand on steady surface
Maintain steady travel speed
Reduce CTWD

troubleshooting.description: Base material melts away, leaving a hole in the weld

causes_and_solutions.solution: Reduce current and/or wire feed speed
Increase travel speed and ensure that travel speed is kept steady
Reduce wire feed speed

troubleshooting.solution: Increase current or weld more slowly
Decrease current or weld more quickly

troubleshooting.description: Weld drools on top and underneath or falls through entirely, making a hole.

causes_solutions.solution: Increase welding speed and ensure that welding speed is kept steady

troubleshooting.description: Weld does not contact the joint fully, just on the surface.

causes_solutions.solution: Keep arc on leading edge of weld puddle. Hold torch at proper angles
Slow down so fill material has time to melt into the weld location. Increase current
Bevel thick workpieces, allow slight gap, and weld in several passes

troubleshooting.description: Gaps present between weld and previous bead or between weld and workpiece.

causes_solutions.solution: Place stringer bead at correct place in joint. Adjust workpiece position or weld angle to permit proper welding to bottom of piece. Keep arc on leading edge of weld puddle. Hold Electrode and fill material at proper angles
Clean workpiece down to bare metal
Clamp workpieces securely. Make tack welds to hold workpieces
Weld a small portion and allow to cool before proceeding. Increase weld speed

troubleshooting.description: Slag is a necessary part of a stick weld. It shields the weld from impurities. Clean off the slag with the Chipping Hammer and Wire Brush after welding.
Small cavities or holes in the bead.

causes_and_solutions.solution: Clean workplace down to bare metal. Make certain that fill material and Electrode are clean and free from oil, coatings, and other residues.
Maintain steady weld speed.
Use two hands or rest hand on steady surface.
Maintain steady weld speed.

troubleshooting.description: Fine spatter is normal. Spatter that is grainy and large is a problem.

causes_and_solutions.solution: Clean workplace down to bare metal. Make certain that fill material and Electrode are clean and free from oil, coatings, and other residues.

troubleshooting.description: Base material melts away, leaving a hole in the weld.

causes_and_solutions.solution: Reduce current.
Increase welding speed and ensure the welding speed is kept steady.
Reduce amount of fill material.

troubleshooting.problem: Wire Feed Motor Runs but Wire Does Not Feed Properly
causes: Damaged MIG Gun, cable, or liner assembly
solutions: Increase wire feed pressure properly. Follow step 27 on page 17
Flip roll to correct size. Follow the Feed Roller instructions on page 12
Have a qualified technician inspect these parts and replace as necessary
Loosen Feed Tensioner so it applies only enough pressure to prevent continued spinning after the Gun Trigger is released

troubleshooting.problem: Wire Creates a Bird's Nest During Operation
causes: MIG Gun Cable Connector not fully inserted into Wire Feed mechanism
solutions: Adjust wire feed pressure properly. Follow step 27 on page 17
Replace with the proper tip for wire used
Insert Gun Cable Connector properly. Follow steps 13 and 14 on page 13
Have a qualified technician inspect and repair/replace as necessary

causes: Gun cable is severely bent and Wire Feed mechanism cannot feed wire
Gun liner is clogged or worn
Gun liner is too small for welding wire being used
Wire is tangled on the spool
Wire is not making contact with Feed Rollers
Feed Roller is not making enough contact with wire or is crushing flux-cored wire
solutions: Straighten Gun cable
Check gun liner for obstruction. Replace if necessary
Check that gun liner is correct size for wire
Check wire for cross winding or tangled spool
Check Feed Rollers and ensure correct groove for wire diameter is being used
Check Feed Tensioner and ensure it is set properly

causes: Incorrect Contact Tip or liner size or excessive wear
Loose MIG Gun cable or ground cable
Damaged MIG Gun or loose connection within gun
Incorrect polarity for process being run
Gas coverage may be insufficient or too high
solutions: See first Troubleshooting section above
Replace with the proper tip or liner size for wire used
Adjust wire feed speed to achieve a more stable arc
Check to ensure that all connections are tight
Have a qualified technician inspect and repair/replace as necessary
Ensure polarity is correct for operation: DCEP for MIG welding and DCEN for Flux-Cored self-shielded welding
Ensure gas flow rate is set according to Settings Chart. Make sure MIG Gun Cable Connector is fully inserted into Wire Feed mechanism with no O-Rings exposed
Check the ground clamp connection to the workpiece and machine. Ensure the MIG Gun is properly secured

causes: Improper gauge or length of cord
solutions: Check the line voltage and, if insufficient, have a licensed electrician remedy the situation
Do not use an extension cord on this Welder. Use only one of the supplied power cords for this Welder or an identical replacement cord
Switch current to proper setting for metal thickness

troubleshooting.problem: Welder Does Not Function When Switched On
causes: Circuit supplies insufficient input voltage or amperage
Machine is in low- or over-voltage protection
Machine is in the incorrect mode
solutions: If a warning screen appears in the LCD Display window, Welder may have overheated and shut down. Stop and wait with the Power Switch ON for the Welder to cool. The Welder automatically returns to service after cooling off. Reduce duration or frequency of welding periods to help reduce wear on the Welder. Refer to Duty Cycle (Duration of Use) on page 19

Verify that the circuit is designed to supply the required input voltage and amperage as detailed on the Specifications table. If a warning screen appears in the LCD Display window, check the input voltage to ensure it is within specified range
Ensure the gun connection is properly seated on machine. Qualified technician must check and repair/replace Trigger
Check input voltage and ensure it falls within the specified range. If input voltage is correct, press Reset Button on back of machine
Ensure the correct process has been selected

troubleshooting.problem: LCD Display Does Not Light When Welder is Switched On
causes: Unit is not connected to outlet properly
Plug does not have correct rating
Circuit breaker has tripped due to high input amperage
Input Power Cord is not seated properly
solutions: Verify the voltage at the outlet and the connection to the outlet
Check circuit breaker/GFCI devices; if any are tripped, determine and remedy cause before resetting
Make sure installed plug is correct rating. See Specifications on page 7
Press Reset Button on back of machine to reset circuit breaker
Ensure the twist lock input Power Cord is fully secured

troubleshooting.problem: Wire Feeds, but Arc Does Not Ignite
solutions: Make certain that the workpiece is contacted properly by the Ground Clamp and that the workpiece is properly cleaned near the ground clamp and the welding location
Verify that Contact Tip is the proper size for welding wire. If needed, replace Contact Tip with proper size and type
Check that the hole in the tip is not deformed or enlarged. If needed, replace Contact Tip with proper size and type
Properly clean Contact Tip

causes: Not enough or too much shielding gas
Gun is being used too far away from workpiece
Polarity is incorrect for the application
Dirty welding wire is introducing contamination into the weld
solutions: Check gas bottle and replenish as necessary
Check gas flow regulator to ensure proper flow
Clean workpiece down to bare metal
Check CTWD (contact tip to work distance) for the proper procedure
Check the polarity and ensure it is DCEP for MIG and DCEN for Flux-Cored
Make certain that welding wire is clean and free of rust and residues

troubleshooting.problem: Welder Does Not Function When Switched On
possible_causes: Ground Clamp not attached to workpiece
likely_solutions: Reduce duration or frequency of welding periods to help reduce wear on the welder. Refer to Duty Cycle (Duration of Use) on page 29
Qualified technician must check and secure/replace Trigger

troubleshooting.problem: LCD Display Does Not Light When Welder is Switched On
possible_causes: Unit is not connected to outlet properly
likely_solutions: Verify the voltage at the outlet and the connection to the outlet

Check circuit breaker/GFCI devices; if any are tripped, determine and remedy cause before resetting. Verify that the circuit is designed to supply the required input amperage as detailed in Specifications on page 7

possible_causes: Improper gauge or length of cord
likely_solutions: Check the line voltage and, if insufficient, have a licensed electrician remedy the situation
Do not use an extension cord on this Welder. Use only one of the supplied power cords for this Welder or an identical replacement cord

possible_causes: Loose electrode cable or ground cable
Damaged electrode holder or loose connection within electrode holder
likely_solutions: Check to ensure that all connections are tight
Have a qualified technician inspect and repair/replace as necessary
Make sure setting matches recommended setting on chart

causes: Shielding gas issues (low flow, leaks, wind)
fixes: Check gas flow rate (25-35 CFH for MIG)
Clean work area thoroughly with acetone or wire brush

causes: Wire speed too fast or slow
fixes: Adjust wire speed to match voltage

causes: Wire tension too tight or loose
Wrong liner size for wire diameter
fixes: Adjust drive roll tension - wire should slip under heavy load
Replace contact tip and clean nozzle
Adjust tension: too tight causes shaving, too loose causes slipping
Angle torch 10-15 degrees in direction of travel
Use lower voltage setting or switch to 120V input
Reduce gap or use backing material

troubleshooting.problem: No arc or arc won't start
causes: Electrode or wire not making contact
fixes: Clean and clamp ground firmly to bare metal
Check polarity matches process (DCEP for MIG, DCEN for TIG)
Check input power and reset breaker
Wait for machine to cool down

causes: Incorrect shielding gas or flow rate
Wind or drafts disturbing gas coverage
fixes: Clean base metal to bright metal
Verify correct gas and flow rate
Use wind screens or move to sheltered area

troubleshooting.problem: Lack of fusion or cold lap

causes: High carbon or alloy content in base metal
fixes: Ensure dry consumables and clean surface
Allow for thermal expansion during cooling
